definition: The ROC curve plots True Positive Rate (TPR = recall) against False Positive Rate (FPR = 1 − specificity) as the classification threshold on predicted scores varies. AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve) summarizes that curve as a single threshold-independent score in [0, 1]: the probability that a randomly chosen positive ranks above a randomly chosen negative. This topic is the canonical home for ROC curves, AUC, and threshold-free ranking evaluation in binary classification.
layman: Imagine a model that scores every customer for fraud risk from 0 to 1. Slide a cutoff from strict to lenient: each cutoff gives a different mix of caught frauds (true positives) and false alarms (false positives). The ROC curve draws that trade-off as a line. AUC is one number summarizing the whole line — how well the model ranks real fraud above innocent transactions, without picking one cutoff. Higher AUC means better ranking; 0.5 is coin-flip random.
content:
## ROC curve and AUC

**ROC** (Receiver Operating Characteristic) and **AUC** (Area Under the Curve) evaluate **binary classifiers** by how well they **rank** positives above negatives — **without committing to one threshold**.

Given a held-out set with true labels **y ∈ {0, 1}** and a **score** **s** per example (probability **ŷ**, SVM **decision_function**, logit, etc.):

1. Sort examples by **s** descending.
2. Walk thresholds from **+∞** down to **−∞** (equivalently, sweep every distinct score).
3. At each threshold **t**, predict class 1 iff **s ≥ t**.
4. Compute **TPR** and **FPR** from the resulting counts.
5. Plot **TPR** (y-axis) vs **FPR** (x-axis) — that is the **ROC curve**.
6. **AUC** = area under that curve (trapezoidal rule in practice).

This topic is the **canonical home** for the **ROC curve**, **AUC**, and **threshold-independent ranking** metrics. **Confusion Matrix** owns the 2×2 layout at a **fixed** threshold; **Precision/Recall/F1** owns **single-threshold** classification metrics; **Logistic Regression** and **Support Vector Machines** produce the **scores** ROC consumes.

### Rates at one threshold

From counts **TP, FP, TN, FN** (see **Confusion Matrix** for definitions):

| Rate | Formula | Also called |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **TPR** | **TP / (TP + FN)** | Sensitivity, recall, hit rate |
| **FPR** | **FP / (FP + TN)** | 1 − specificity, fall-out |

**TPR** asks: *of all actual positives, what fraction did we flag?*

**FPR** asks: *of all actual negatives, what fraction did we falsely flag?*

ROC traces **(FPR, TPR)** pairs as the threshold moves — not precision or F1 at one cutoff.

### Reading the ROC curve

- **(0, 0)** — threshold so high nothing is positive: **TPR = 0**, **FPR = 0**.
- **(1, 1)** — threshold so low everything is positive: **TPR = 1**, **FPR = 1**.
- **Diagonal y = x** — scores carry **no ranking signal** (random classifier); **AUC = 0.5**.
- **Upper-left bow** — high **TPR** at low **FPR** — desirable; curve hugs the top-left corner.
- **Perfect separator** — step from **(0, 0)** to **(0, 1)** to **(1, 1)** — **AUC = 1**.

### AUC interpretation

**AUC ∈ [0, 1]** summarizes separability across **all** thresholds:

| AUC | Typical reading |
| --- | --- |
| **0.5** | No better than random ranking |
| **0.7–0.8** | Acceptable for many applications |
| **0.8–0.9** | Good |
| **> 0.9** | Excellent (watch overfitting / leakage) |

**Probabilistic interpretation (equivalent for continuous scores):**

**AUC = P(score(positive) > score(negative))** for a random positive–negative pair (ties handled at 0.5 in standard definitions).

So AUC measures **ranking quality**, not calibration. A model can have great AUC but poor probability calibration — use **Brier score** or calibration plots separately if calibrated **ŷ** matter.

### Why threshold-independent?

Deployment thresholds depend on **business costs** (false alarm vs missed fraud). **Accuracy** and **F1** at 0.5 embed one arbitrary cutoff. **ROC-AUC** answers: *if we could tune the threshold later, how separable are the classes by this scorer?* — ideal for **comparing models** before picking an operating point.

After choosing AUC on validation, pick a threshold via cost analysis, ROC operating point, or **precision-recall** trade-offs (see **Precision/Recall/F1** when the positive class is rare).

### Requirements and limitations

**Needs:**
- **Binary** labels (or one-vs-rest extensions per class).
- A **real-valued score** per example — higher should mean more likely positive.

**Caveats:**
- **Class imbalance:** ROC can look optimistic when negatives dominate; **PR-AUC** (precision-recall AUC) often tells a sharper story on rare positives — still threshold-free, but owned alongside **Precision/Recall/F1**, not re-derived here.
- **AUC hides** the exact **FPR** at the **TPR** you need — always inspect the curve or a confusion matrix at your deployment threshold.
- **Not for regression** or multi-label without reduction to binary tasks.

### Worked micro-example

Four examples, scores **s**, labels **y**:

| s | y |
| --- | --- |
| 0.9 | 1 |
| 0.7 | 1 |
| 0.4 | 0 |
| 0.2 | 0 |

Perfect ranking (all positives above all negatives) → ROC passes **(0,0) → (0,1) → (1,1)** → **AUC = 1**.

Swap scores on the two positives only → some negatives rank above some positives → **AUC < 1**.

### Summary map

| Concept | Canonical home |
| --- | --- |
| **ROC curve, AUC, ranking metric** | **This topic** |
| TP, FP, TN, FN at one threshold | **Confusion Matrix** |
| Precision, recall, F1 at one threshold | **Precision/Recall/F1** |
| Probabilistic scores (sigmoid ŷ) | **Logistic Regression** |
| Margin scores (decision_function) | **Support Vector Machines** |
keyPoints: ROC plots TPR vs FPR as the classification threshold on scores sweeps from high to low.
AUC ∈ [0, 1] summarizes ranking: P(score_positive > score_negative); 0.5 = random, 1 = perfect separation.
Threshold-independent — compare models before choosing a deployment cutoff.
Requires binary labels and a real-valued score; does not judge calibration by itself.
Confusion matrix counts at one threshold and precision/recall at one cutoff live in sibling topics.
